<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Checking project structure**

I confirmed the project uses Next.js 13+ with app/router and pages directories, where / hits app/page.tsx. 

**Checking browser compatibility**

The 'events' package is a Node core module and may not be available in browser bundles. The 'events' package is available for browser use and can be imported directly. The 'events' package works in the browser and can be imported directly for EventEmitter functionality. 

I confirmed that the 'events' module is a Node built-in and Next.js likely handles it automatically, so no explicit dependency needed. </details></think>

Phase 1.1 — Project Setup is complete. Summary:

## Completed Tasks

### 1. Dependencies installed
- `rot-js`, `zustand`, `uuid` (runtime)
- `@types/uuid` (dev)

### 2. Folder structure and files

Created the structure from PLAN.md:

```
src/
├── app/
│   ├── layout.tsx
│   └── page.tsx
├── components/
│   ├── GameContainer.tsx, HUD.tsx, DialogueBox.tsx
│   ├── LibraryShelf.tsx, BookDetail.tsx, AccessibleLog.tsx
├── game/
│   ├── scenes/
│   │   ├── BootScene.ts, ExploreScene.ts, ShipScene.ts
│   ├── entities/
│   │   ├── Player.ts, NPC.ts, BookPickup.ts
│   ├── systems/
│   │   ├── GridMovement.ts, MapGenerator.ts, FOVSystem.ts, Interaction.ts
│   └── EventBridge.ts
├── store/gameStore.ts
├── data/
│   ├── books.ts, npcs.ts, journalEntries.ts, tilesets.ts
├── types/
│   ├── game.ts, books.ts, store.ts
├── config/gameConfig.ts
└── utils/
    ├── mapUtils.ts, gutenberg.ts
```

### 3. `EventBridge.ts`

Typed EventEmitter singleton for Phaser ↔ React:

- Typed events: `player-moved`, `interaction-available`, `interaction-triggered`, `book-found`, `npc-dialogue`, `area-entered`, `close-dialogue`, `open-inventory`
- Typed `emit`, `on`, `once`, `off` for type-safe event payloads

### 4. `gameConfig.ts`

```typescript
TILE_SIZE: 32
MAP_WIDTH: 50        // tiles
MAP_HEIGHT: 50       // tiles
PLAYER_MOVE_DURATION: 150  // ms
```

### 5. App Router setup

The project now uses the App Router instead of the Pages Router. `GameContainer` mounts `PhaserGame` so the Phaser game still renders.

The build passes. Run `npm run dev` and open http://localhost:8080 to confirm the Phaser game loads; new scenes are stubs and the template (Boot → Preloader → MainMenu → Game) will run until later phases wire in the new scenes.

| Web search results for query: zustand 5 persist middleware localStorage 2024 |  | Links:<br/>1. [Persisting store data - Zustand](https://docs.pmnd.rs/zustand/integrations/persisting-store-data)<br/>2. [How to Use Zustand in React (With Local Storage Persistence)](https://medium.com/@jalish.dev/how-to-use-zustand-in-react-with-local-storage-persistence-fd67ab0cc5a0)<br/>3. [Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand/discussions/2805)<br/>4. [Using Persist from Zustand for state management in localStorage](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/77520513/using-persist-from-zustand-for-state-management-in-localstorage)<br/>5. [Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand/discussions/2619)<br/><br/># Zustand 5 Persist Middleware with localStorage<br/><br/>## Basic Setup<br/><br/>Zustand's persist middleware enables storing state in localStorage (or other storage backends like AsyncStorage and IndexedDB). By default, it uses localStorage.[1]<br/><br/>**Simple example:**<br/>```ts<br/>import \{ create \} from 'zustand'<br/>import \{ persist, createJSONStorage \} from 'zustand/middleware'<br/><br/>export const useBearStore = create()(<br/>  persist(<br/>    (set, get) => (\{<br/>      bears: 0,<br/>      addABear: () => set(\{ bears: get().bears + 1 \}),<br/>    \}),<br/>    \{<br/>      name: 'food-storage', // unique key for storage<br/>      storage: createJSONStorage(() => localStorage), // optional, default<br/>    \},<br/>  ),<br/>)<br/>```<br/><br/>## Key Configuration Options<br/><br/>**name** (required): The unique key for storing state in localStorage.[1]<br/><br/>**storage**: Specify which storage to use (defaults to localStorage). Can use `createJSONStorage()` helper for custom storage implementations.[1]<br/><br/>**partialize**: Select specific state fields to persist instead of all state.[1] For example, to persist only the `foo` field:<br/>```ts<br/>partialize: (state) => (\{ foo: state.foo \})<br/>```<br/><br/>**onRehydrateStorage**: Listen function called when storage is hydrated, useful for handling errors during rehydration.[1]<br/><br/>**version**: Introduce breaking changes by incrementing the version number for state migrations.[1]<br/><br/>## Important v5 Change<br/><br/>In Zustand v5, the persist middleware no longer stores the item at store creation. You must manually set the state after store creation if needed.[3] This is particularly important with async storage to avoid overwriting persisted values on app reload. |

</details>
</tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Fixing persist behavior**

I'm adjusting the persist setup to ensure actions aren't overwritten during rehydration. 

**Merging persisted state**

I'm merging persisted state objects into the full state while preserving initial values like `session` and `actions`. I'm using the store's persist API instead of manual localStorage writes to maintain consistency with the persist middleware format. I'm implementing auto-save behavior by leveraging the persist middleware's built-in save mechanism instead of manual localStorage writes. </details></think>

[Fix Next.js 14 hydration error with Zustand state management](https://medium.com/@koalamango/fix-next-js-hydration-error-with-zustand-state-management-0ce51a0176ad)<br/>3. [persist - Zustand](https://zustand.docs.pmnd.rs/middlewares/persist)<br/>4. [NextJS Zustand persist state](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72649255/nextjs-zustand-persist-state)<br/>5. [NextJS Zustand persist state](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72649255/nextjs-zustand-persist-state/73705292)<br/><br/># Zustand Persist with skipHydration for Next.js SSR<br/><br/>## The Core Problem<br/><br/>Using Zustand's `persist` middleware in Next.js causes hydration errors because the server and client render different initial states.[4] The server renders with default state, while the client hydrates with persisted state from localStorage, creating a mismatch.[2]<br/><br/>## The Solution: skipHydration<br/><br/>The `persist` middleware has a `skipHydration` option that prevents automatic rehydration on initialization.[3] When set to `true`, you manually control when the store rehydrates, allowing you to rehydrate only on the client after mounting.<br/><br/>**Key configuration:**<br/>```<br/>skipHydration: Defaults to false. If true, the middleware won't automatically rehydrate <br/>the state on initialization. Use the rehydrate function manually in this case. <br/>This is useful for server-side rendering (SSR) applications.[3]<br/>```<br/><br/>## Best Practices for Next.js<br/><br/>The recommended approach is to create stores per-request rather than as global variables, use Context providers to share state, and ensure the store initializes identically on server and client before rehydration occurs.[1]<br/><br/>For App Router applications, you should wrap persisted stores in a client component (`'use client'`) and manually trigger rehydration after the component mounts to prevent hydration mismatches.[2] |

</details>
</tool-use>

Phase 1.2 is done. Summary:

## Phase 1.2 — Zustand Store (Convex-Shaped)

### 1. Types (`src/types/`)

**`game.ts`**
- `Position`, `Direction`

**`books.ts`**
- `BookFragment`: `id`, `bookId`, `label`, `order`, `text`

**`store.ts`**
- `DialogueLine`, `JournalEntry`
- `PlayerState`, `ExplorationState`, `PersistedState`, `SessionState`, `GamePhase`
- `GameState`, `GameActions`

### 2. Store (`src/store/gameStore.ts`)

- Same shape as the planned Convex schema
- Persists only `player`, `library`, `exploration` (via `partialize`)
- Session state (`currentDialogue`, `currentJournal`, etc.) is not persisted
- All actions implemented (they can be swapped for Convex mutations later)
- Convex schema comment block at the top for migration

### 3. LocalStorage persistence

- Uses Zustand `persist` middleware with `createJSONStorage`
- `partialize` limits persisted data to `player`, `library`, `exploration`
- `saveToLocalStorage`: no-op (persist auto-saves; placeholder for Convex sync)
- `loadFromLocalStorage`: calls `persist.rehydrate()` for manual reload

### 4. Convex migration

The store is structured for a simple Convex migration:

- **Persisted tables**: `player`, `library`, `exploration` map to Convex tables
- **Session**: stays client-side or handled by Convex subscriptions
- **Actions**: logic can be moved into Convex mutations without changing call sites
- **Schema comment**: documents the planned Convex tables and fields

Half-Walls (perfect for layered depth)\n\n50 Props(Variants included): signs, benches, traffic lights and more\n\nBonus Background for mockups or game layering\n\nOrganized PNGs + source files (Plus source pack)\n\n---\n\nDetails:\n\n32×32 grid-based tiles\n\nPixel perfect &amp; I am making a game set in a war-torn city and I'm hoping to find things like old broken down houses, shops etc\n\n[Reply](https://itch.io/login?return_to=https%3A%2F%2Fpixkari.itch.io%2Fpost-apocalyptic-assets&amp;amp;intent=community)\n\n[Pixkari](https://itch.io/profile/pixkari) [311 days ago](https://itch.io/post/12690791)(1 edit)\n\nHi, the pack contains mostly props for exteriors with different variants and some vegetation, it does contain simple tilemaps for the road and for brick buildings for the background but I'm afraid it doesn't contain entire buildings\n\n[Reply](https://itch.io/login?return_to=https%3A%2F%2Fpixkari.itch.io%2Fpost-apocalyptic-assets&amp;amp;intent=community)\n\n[itch.io](https://itch.io/)· [View all by Pixkari](https://pixkari.itch.io/)· [Report](javascript:void(0);)· [Embed](javascript:void(0);)"

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Generating runtime tileset**

I'm creating a programmatic tileset using Phaser's graphics API to avoid downloading the Pixkari demo. I'm using Phaser's `createCanvas` method to generate the tileset programmatically. I'm creating a minimal 96x96 PNG tileset with 4 tile types as colored squares. I'm generating a 64x64 runtime tileset using Phaser's Graphics API in the Boot scene. I'm creating a minimal 64x64 PNG tileset with base64 data URL for embedding. I'm switching to using the Kenney Roguelike pack assets and will structure the code to expect assets in `public/assets/`. </details></think>

[Phaser - how to select a Tiled map layer and set it as collision layer](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33605657/phaser-how-to-select-a-tiled-map-layer-and-set-it-as-collision-layer)<br/>3. [tilemaps/multiple-layer-collision](https://samme.github.io/phaser-examples-mirror/tilemaps/multiple-layer-collision.html)<br/>4. [](https://dustinpfister.github.io/2018/09/26/phaser-tilemap-set-collision-by-exclusion/)<br/>5. [Phaser.Tilemaps.Components \| Phaser Help](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/3.88.2/namespace/tilemaps-components)<br/><br/># Phaser 3 setCollision Tilemap Layer by Index<br/><br/>To set collision on a tilemap layer by tile index in Phaser 3, use the `setCollision()` method on the tilemap object. Here are the main approaches:[1]<br/><br/>## Methods for Setting Collision<br/><br/>**Single tile index:**<br/>```javascript<br/>this.map.setCollision(11);<br/>```<br/><br/>**Range of tile indexes:**<br/>```javascript<br/>this.map.setCollisionBetween(14, 16);<br/>```<br/><br/>**Multiple tile indexes (array):**<br/>```javascript<br/>this.map.setCollision([20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 27, 28, 29, 33, 39, 40]);<br/>```<br/><br/>**All tiles except specified indexes:**<br/>```javascript<br/>this.map.setCollisionByExclusion([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]);<br/>```<br/><br/>## Layer-Specific Collision<br/><br/>To set collision for a specific layer, pass the layer as the last parameter:[3]<br/><br/>```javascript<br/>this.map.setCollisionByExclusion([excludedIndex], true, layer);<br/>```<br/><br/>This allows different collision settings for different layers in a multi-layer tilemap.[3]<br/><br/>After setting collision, you can visualize it using `renderDebug()` to verify which tiles are marked as colliding.[1] |

</details>

[Generating a texture with graphics to use with sprites in phaser ce \| Dustin John Pfister at github pages](https://dustinpfister.github.io/2018/11/20/phaser-graphics-generate-texture/)<br/><br/># Phaser 3 Graphics generateTexture<br/><br/>To create a texture from graphics in Phaser 3, use the `generateTexture()` method on a Graphics object.[1]<br/><br/>## Basic Usage<br/><br/>Call `generateTexture()` with a texture key and dimensions:[1]<br/><br/>```javascript<br/>const graphics = this.make.graphics().fillStyle(0x00ff00).fillRect(0, 0, 800, 100);<br/>graphics.generateTexture('hudbar', 800, 100);<br/>graphics.destroy();<br/>this.add.image(400, 300, 'hudbar');<br/>```<br/><br/>The method takes three parameters:<br/>- **Texture key** (string): A unique identifier for the generated texture<br/>- **Width** (number): The width of the texture in pixels<br/>- **Height** (number): The height of the texture in pixels<br/><br/>## Workflow<br/><br/>1. Create a Graphics object using `this.make.graphics()`<br/>2. Draw on it using standard graphics methods (fillStyle, fillRect, lineStyle, etc.)<br/>3. Call `generateTexture(key, width, height)` to convert it to a reusable texture<br/>4. Optionally destroy the graphics object to free resources<br/>5. Use the texture key with `this.add.image()` or other display objects[1][2]<br/><br/>The generated texture is registered in the texture manager and can be used anywhere a texture key is accepted. This allows you to dynamically create textures without relying on pre-loaded image assets.[2] |

Phase 1.3 is done. Summary:

## Phase 1.3 — Basic Tilemap Rendering

### 1. Procedural tileset (runtime)

No external tiles are required. `BootScene` generates a procedural tileset at runtime with a post‑apocalyptic palette:

- **Ground**: stone floor, grass, dirt, stone floor (interior)
- **Walls**: dark rubble, lighter rubble
- **Decoration**: vines, debris

Also generated:

- Book pickup (yellow circle)
- Transporter pad (blue outline)
- Player (green square) for camera follow
- NPC (orange square)

### 2. BootScene

- Generates the procedural tileset in `preload()`
- Starts `ExploreScene` immediately (no preloader for now)

### 3. Static test tilemap in ExploreScene

- **Ground**: library rooms (stone floor), courtyard (grass), corridors (floor), outdoor areas (grass/dirt)
- **Walls**: borders, room outlines, walls at indices 4–5 marked as collidable
- **Decoration**: vines and debris on ~2% of non‑wall tiles

### 4. Phaser setup

- Three layers: ground → walls → decoration
- Wall layer uses `setCollision([4, 5])` for rubble
- World bounds: 50×50 tiles × 32px = 1600×1600 px
- Camera follows a placeholder player sprite with lerp (0.08, 0.08)
- Spawn at (10, 10) in the “Ruined Library Wing”

### 5. Game entry

- `main.ts` updated to use `BootScene` → `ExploreScene` → `ShipScene` instead of the template scenes
- Background color set to `#1a1a2e`

### 6. Assets

- `public/assets/README.md` added with notes for optional itch.io/Kenney tilesets

### Files created/updated

- `src/game/scenes/BootScene.ts` – procedural asset creation
- `src/game/scenes/ExploreScene.ts` – tilemap, layers, camera, player placeholder
- `src/game/main.ts` – scene list, physics config
- `src/data/tilesets.ts` – tile indices
- `src/utils/mapUtils.ts` – `createStaticTestMap()`
- `public/assets/README.md` – asset notes

Run `npm run dev` and open the game to see the tilemap and camera. Phase 1.4 will add grid movement and replace the placeholder player sprite.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Implementing player entity**

I'm adding the Player entity with sprite, grid position, and facing direction. I'm adding the MovementController interface and implementing GridMovementController. I'm creating the Player entity that uses the GridMovementController. 

**Adding collision detection**

I'm implementing collision detection to prevent movement into wall tiles by checking the wall layer's tile indices before tweening. 

**Implementing movement controller**

I'm implementing a MovementController interface to handle grid-based movement validation. I'm wiring up the Player entity with its sprite, grid position, and facing direction, and connecting it to the MovementController. I'm refactoring GridMovement from a component to a system class that directly controls the player entity. I'm implementing the MovementController interface with a tryMove method that handles input, collision detection, and tweening. I'm designing the MovementController interface to decouple player movement logic from the Player class, enabling future swapping of movement types like FreeMovementController. I'm implementing the MovementController interface with attach/detach methods and input handling via scene listeners. 

I'm implementing the MovementController with input handling and tween-based movement logic. </details></think>

[KeyboardManager \| Phaser Help](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/class/input-keyboard-keyboardmanager)<br/><br/># Phaser 3 Keyboard Keydown Callback Parameter<br/><br/>In Phaser 3, the `keydown` event callback receives a single parameter: a native **KeyboardEvent** object.[1]<br/><br/>## Usage<br/><br/>You can listen for keydown events globally or for specific keys:<br/><br/>**Global keydown listener:**<br/>```javascript<br/>this.input.keyboard.on('keydown', event => \{<br/>    console.dir(event);<br/>\});<br/>```<br/><br/>**Specific key listener:**<br/>```javascript<br/>this.input.keyboard.on('keydown-A', event => \{<br/>    console.log('A key pressed');<br/>\});<br/>```<br/><br/>## Parameter Details<br/><br/>The `event` parameter is a native DOM `KeyboardEvent` object.[1] You can inspect it to access information about the key that was pressed, including modifier keys (shift, control, alt, etc.). <br/><br/>Additional related properties available through Key objects include `altKey`, `ctrlKey`, `metaKey`, `shiftKey`, and `keyCode`.[4] |

Phase 1.4 is done. Summary:

## Phase 1.4 — Player Entity & Grid Movement

### 1. `IControllablePlayer` & `MovementController` (`src/types/game.ts`)

- **IControllablePlayer**: `getGridPosition()`, `setGridPosition()`, `setDirection()`, `getSprite()`
- **MovementContext**: `wallLayer`, `mapWidth`, `mapHeight`
- **MovementController**: `attach(scene, player, context)`, `detach()`, so `GridMovement` can be swapped for `FreeMovementController` later

### 2. Player entity (`src/game/entities/Player.ts`)

- Sprite created with a texture key (e.g. `'player'`)
- **Grid position**: `gridX`, `gridY` separate from pixel position
- **Facing direction**: up, down, left, right
- **Visuals**: sprite angle changes with direction (0° right, 90° down, 180° left, -90° up)
- Implements `IControllablePlayer`

### 3. GridMovement (`src/game/systems/GridMovement.ts`)

- Listens for Arrow keys and WASD
- On input: target tile = current tile + direction delta
- **Collision**: checks wall layer via `getTileAt()`; indices 4 and 5 are blocked
- **Bounds**: blocks moves outside 0..mapWidth and 0..mapHeight
- **Tween**: 150ms (from `PLAYER_MOVE_DURATION`)
- **Input lock**: `isMoving` blocks input during tween
- Emits `player-moved` with `{ x, y }`
- Implements `MovementController` with `attach` / `detach`

### 4. ExploreScene changes

- Uses `Player` instead of a raw sprite
- Creates `GridMovement` and attaches it to `Player` and the wall layer
- Subscribes to `player-moved` and syncs the Zustand store
- Camera follows `player.getSprite()`
- Calls `gridMovement.detach()` in `shutdown`

Run `npm run dev` and use the arrow keys or WASD to move; you should see grid movement, wall blocking, and camera following.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Creating interaction system**

I'm implementing the Interaction.ts system to track nearby interactive objects and handle player interactions. I'm defining the interactive object interface and implementing the registry system to track nearby objects. 

**Handling interaction prompts**

I'm implementing the logic to clear interaction prompts when no objects are nearby. I'm integrating the interaction system with GridMovement, ensuring E and Space keys trigger interactions without conflicting with movement controls. 

I'm emitting the 'interaction-available' event for Phase 1.5 while deferring prompt rendering to Phase 1.6. 

**Designing interactive objects**

I'm designing an interactive object interface with id, type, position, and optional label fields. 

**Handling player interactions**

I'm implementing the interaction system that checks tile positions relative to player direction and emits interaction events. </details></think>

---


Phase 1.5 is done. Summary of what was added:

## Phase 1.5 — Interaction System

### 1. Types (`src/types/game.ts`)
- **InteractiveType**: `'book' | 'npc' | 'journal' | 'transporter'`
- **Interactive**: `id`, `type`, `gridX`, `gridY`, optional `label`
- `getDirection()` added to `IControllablePlayer` for the interaction system

### 2. InteractionSystem (`src/game/systems/Interaction.ts`)
- **Register/unregister**: interactives register themselves via `register()` and `unregister()`
- **Proximity**: checks the tile 1 step ahead in the facing direction each frame
- **E & Space**: on press, if an interactive is available, emits `interaction-triggered`
- **Events**: `interaction-available` (with `type` and `label` when facing, or empty when not), `interaction-triggered` (with `type` and `id`)

### 3. ExploreScene updates
- **Transporter pad** at (10, 9) (above spawn) — face up from spawn and use E/Space
- **Book pickup** at (12, 10) (right of spawn) — move right and face it
- **Prompt**: `[E] Transporter pad` / `[E] Book fragment` appears above the player when facing an interactive
- Prompt hides when turning away or moving

### Flow
1. Move to (10, 10), face up (↑) → prompt: `[E] Transporter pad`
2. Press **E** or **Space** → `interaction-triggered` with `type: 'transporter'`
3. Move to (11, 10), face right (→) → prompt: `[E] Book fragment`
4. Press **E** or **Space** → `interaction-triggered` with `type: 'book'`

Phase 1.6 will add the React UI that listens to these events (HUD, AccessibleLog, DialogueBox). For now, the in-canvas prompt shows that interactions work.

---

_**User**_

Okay, on to phase 1.6 please!

---

_**Agent (model claude-4.5-opus-high-thinking, mode Agent)**_

Phase 1.6 is done. Summary:

## Phase 1.6 — React UI Shell

### 1. **GameContainer.tsx**
- Wraps PhaserGame and all overlays
- Subscribes to `interaction-triggered` and opens dialogue for transporter, book, npc, journal
- Uses `game-container` for layout

### 2. **HUD.tsx**
- **Header** with `role="banner"` and `aria-label="Game status"`
- **Title**: "Starship Alexandria"
- **Area name**: from `area-entered` (default: "Ruined Library Wing")
- **Fragment count**: `📚 X/47 fragments recovered` from the store
- Layout: compact bar at the top with amber accents

### 3. **AccessibleLog.tsx**
- **Live region** with `aria-live="polite"`, `aria-atomic="false"`, `role="log"`
- Logs:
  - Player movement
  - Area changes
  - Interaction-available (once per new target to avoid spam)
  - Interaction-triggered
- **Screen reader only** using `.accessible-log` (off-screen clip)

### 4. **DialogueBox.tsx**
- **Dialog** with `role="dialog"`, `aria-modal="true"`
- Text from `session.currentDialogue`
- Advance with Space/Enter; close with Escape
- Multi-line support with one line at a time
- Bottom bar with hint: "Press Space or Enter to continue/close"

### 5. **Styling (`globals.css`)**
- Color variables for HUD and dialogue (dark background, gold accents)
- Crimson Pro for typography (Next.js font)
- Overlay layout for HUD and DialogueBox

### 6. **Layout**
- Crimson Pro loaded in `layout.tsx`
- Main container uses `minHeight: 100vh` for full viewport

Phase 1.7 milestone is now covered: movement, interaction prompt, HUD overlay, and accessible log are in place.

Phase 1.7 is marked complete in `PLAN.md`. Here’s how to play:

---

## How to Play Starship Alexandria (Phase 1)

### Start the game
```bash
npm run dev
```
Open http://localhost:8080 in your browser.

### Controls
- **Move**: **Arrow keys** or **WASD**
- **Interact**: **E** or **Space** when facing an object

### What you can do
1. **Move**
   - Grid-based movement with 150ms tweens
   - Walls are blocked; the camera follows you smoothly

2. **Transporter pad**
   - One tile above spawn
   - From (10, 10), move **↑** to face it
   - Prompt: **[E] Transporter pad**
   - Press **E** → dialogue: “Beam up to Alexandria? (Not implemented yet.)”
   - Use **Space** or **Enter** to close the dialogue

3. **Book fragment**
   - At (12, 10), to the right of spawn
   - Move **→** twice, then face right to look at the yellow glow
   - Prompt: **[E] Book fragment**
   - Press **E** → dialogue: “You found a book fragment.”

4. **HUD**
   - Top bar shows “Starship Alexandria”, area name, and fragment count
   - Current area: “Ruined Library Wing”

5. **Accessible log**
   - Events are logged for screen readers
   - Events include movement, area changes, and interactions

### Layout
- **Library wing** (stone floor) around spawn
- **Corridors** and **courtyard** (grass) in other sections
- Dark rubble tiles are walls
- Random rubble, vines, and debris across the map

---


Here’s how to fix it:

## Fix for ENOENT error

The error occurs when Next.js expects compiled files in `dist/` that aren’t there. Do a clean restart:

### 1. Stop the dev server
Use **Ctrl+C** in the terminal where `npm run dev` is running.

### 2. Kill anything using port 8080 (if needed)
```bash
lsof -ti:8080 | xargs kill -9
```

### 3. Clean build artifacts and restart
```bash
npm run clean && npm run dev
```

Or manually:
```bash
rm -rf dist .next
npm run dev
```

A `clean` script is already in `package.json`, so you can use `npm run clean` anytime you see odd build errors.

---

**Cause:** With `distDir: 'dist'`, Next.js uses `dist/` for dev output. A stale or partial build plus a refresh can make it request `dist/server/app/page.js` before it’s been compiled, leading to the ENOENT error. Removing `dist` and `.next` and restarting forces a fresh build.

---

Here’s what changed:

## Fixes applied

### 1. **Prompt only when stopped**
- Added a `player-moving` EventBridge event when movement starts.
- `InteractionSystem` now tracks movement and hides the prompt during the tween.
- The prompt appears again only after you’ve stopped on a tile.

### 2. **Prompt when standing on an interactive**
- The system checks both:
  - The tile in front of the player
  - The tile the player is standing on
- So when you land on the transporter, the prompt stays visible because you’re on the transporter tile.

Result:  
- While moving: no prompt  
- After landing on the transporter: prompt shows  
- Same for other interactives (e.g. the book)

---

Phase 1 is done and the project is ready for Phase 2. Summary:

---

## Phase 1 status

| Section | Status | Notes |
|--------|--------|------|
| **1.1 Project Setup** | ✅ | Template, deps (rot-js, zustand, uuid), folder structure, EventBridge, gameConfig |
| **1.2 Zustand Store** | ✅ | GameState, localStorage persist, Convex schema comment |
| **1.3 Tilemap Rendering** | ✅ | BootScene (procedural tiles), static map, layers, camera, bounds |
| **1.4 Player & GridMovement** | ✅ | Player entity, MovementController interface, collision, tweens |
| **1.5 Interaction System** | ✅ | Proximity + facing, E/Space, types, movement-aware prompt |
| **1.6 React UI Shell** | ✅ | GameContainer, HUD, AccessibleLog (sr-only), DialogueBox |
| **1.7 Milestone** | ✅ | Movement, collision, interaction prompt, HUD, accessible log |

---

## Phase 2 readiness

- **rot-js** is installed for procedural map generation
- **MapGenerator.ts**, **FOVSystem.ts**, **utils/mapUtils.ts** exist as stubs from 1.1
- **ExploreScene** uses `createStaticTestMap()` — Phase 2.1 will replace this with `MapGenerator`
- **gameConfig** has MAP_WIDTH, MAP_HEIGHT, TILE_SIZE for map generation

---

## Other checks

- Build completes successfully
- PLAN.md checklist is up to date
- `npm run clean` is available for build issues

Phase 2 can start when you’re ready.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Creating map generator**

I'm implementing MapGenerator.ts with rot.js to create 50x50 tilemaps using Digger and Cellular algorithms. 

**Generating tilemap**

I'm creating dynamic tilemaps in Phaser using rot.js Digger algorithm. I'm using ROT.Map.Digger for dungeon-like interior ruins and ROT.Map.Cellular for organic outdoor environments. 

I'll use ROT.Map.Digger for the interior ruins with a 50x50 map size. I'll use ROT.Map.Digger for the main dungeon layout and reserve cellular automata for overgrown outdoor areas. 

I'll use ROT.Map.Digger for the full map generation including overgrown areas, simplifying the approach by avoiding blending multiple generators. I'll implement a hybrid approach using ROT.Map.Digger for interior ruins and ROT.Map.Cellular for overgrown outdoor areas. I'm implementing the Digger algorithm for interior ruins with a plan to add Cellular for outdoor areas later. </details></think>

<details>
            <summary>Tool use: **web_search** • Searched web `undefined` • **1** results</summary>
        
| Title | URL | Chunk |
|-------|-----|-------|
| Web search results for query: rot.js ROT.Map.Digger create getRooms API |  | Links:<br/>1. [map/digger \| rot.js](https://ondras.github.io/rot.js/doc/modules/map_digger.html)<br/>2. [Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/ondras/rot.js)<br/>3. [Rot.js tutorial, part 1](https://roguebasin.com/?title=Rot.js_tutorial,_part_1)<br/>4. [Reference](https://paulofmandown.github.io/rotLove/)<br/>5. [rot.js](https://ondras.github.io/rot.js/doc/)<br/><br/># ROT.Map.Digger API Summary<br/><br/>ROT.Map.Digger is a dungeon map generator in the rot.js library.[1] <br/><br/>## Key Methods<br/><br/>**create()** - The primary method used to generate a dungeon map. It accepts a callback function that gets called repeatedly during the generation process.[3] The callback receives parameters `(x, y, value)` where the value indicates whether a cell is a wall or floor tile.[3]<br/><br/>**getRooms()** - This method retrieves the rooms that were generated during dungeon creation.[1]<br/><br/>## Basic Usage<br/><br/>To use the Digger, instantiate it and pass a callback to the `create()` method:[3]<br/><br/>```javascript<br/>var digger = new ROT.Map.Digger();<br/>var digCallback = function(x, y, value) \{<br/>    if (value) \{ return; \} // do not store walls<br/>    var key = x + "," + y;<br/>    this.map[key] = ".";<br/>\}<br/>digger.create(digCallback.bind(this));<br/>```<br/><br/>The callback-based design allows you to define custom handling for each cell generated, rather than working with a pre-defined data structure.[3] |

</details>
</tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Mapping coordinates**

I'm mapping the coordinate system where x=column, y=row and 0=empty, 1=wall, 2=door in the tilemap. I'm switching to column-major order for the tilemap data structure, using `data[y][x]` format. I'm transposing the map data to use column-major order with `ground[y][x]` format. </details></think>

Phase 2.1 is done. Summary of what changed:

## Phase 2.1 — Procedural Map Generation

### 1. **MapGenerator.ts** (new)
- Uses **ROT.Map.Digger** for interior ruins (rooms + corridors)
- Produces a 50×50 tile map as a 2D array
- Maps `0` (open) → floor/stone floor (random)
- Maps `1` (wall) → wall/rubble (random)
- **Decoration pass:**
  - Vines on ~3% of floor tiles
  - Debris on ~25% of floor tiles next to walls
- **Room metadata:**
  - Uses `getRooms()` for room rectangles
  - Assigns random names: "library wing", "reading room", "archives", "courtyard", "stacks", "rotunda", "vestibule", "study", "scriptorium"
  - Exports `getRoomAt(x, y)` for room lookup

### 2. **ExploreScene**
- Calls `generateMap()` instead of `createStaticTestMap()`
- Spawn at first room center
- Transporter at spawn
- Book on a random open tile (not spawn)
- Emits **area-entered** with room name when entering a room or corridor
- HUD/accessible log receive area names

### 3. **Deferred**
- ROT.Map.Cellular for outdoor areas (Digger covers current needs)
- Light-source tiles (none in tileset)

### Current behavior
- Each load generates a new map
- Moving between rooms updates the HUD with the current room
- Corridors appear as "corridor"

---

_**User**_

Okay, please implement phase 2.2. This will be a milestone. After this I'll do some testing and possibly give feedback.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Implementing fov system**

I'm implementing the FOV system using ROT.js shadowcasting algorithm with three tile states. 

**Tracking explored tiles**

I'm adding explored tiles tracking to the store with session-based persistence for visited maps. I'm adding exploredTiles to the session state for in-session persistence. I'm using Record<mapId, string[]> for exploredTiles to maintain serializable state in Zustand. 

**Storing explored tiles**

I'm moving explored tiles to session state since they should persist within a play session. I'm adding `exploredTilesByMap` to session state to track explored tiles per map. </details></think>

| Title | URL | Chunk |
|-------|-----|-------|
| Web search results for query: Phaser 3 tilemap layer set tile alpha per tile |  | Links:<br/>1. [Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer - Phaser 3 API Documentation](https://newdocs.phaser.io/docs/3.52.0/Phaser.Tilemaps.TilemapLayer)<br/>2. [Tile \| Phaser Help](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/class/tilemaps-tile)<br/>3. [Tilemap \| Phaser Help](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/class/tilemaps-tilemap)<br/>4. [Types.Tilemaps \| Phaser Help](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/4.0.0-rc.6/typedef/types-tilemaps)<br/><br/># Setting Per-Tile Alpha in Phaser 3 Tilemaps<br/><br/>In Phaser 3, you can set alpha (transparency) on individual tiles using the **Tile object's alpha property**.[2]<br/><br/>Each tile in a tilemap is represented by a `Phaser.Tilemaps.Tile` object, which extends `Phaser.GameObjects.Components.AlphaSingle`.[2] This means individual tiles have an `alpha` property that you can modify.<br/><br/>To set alpha for a specific tile:<br/><br/>1. Get the tile from the tilemap layer using methods like `getTileAt(x, y)`<br/>2. Set the tile's `alpha` property to a value between 0 (fully transparent) and 1 (fully opaque)<br/><br/>Example:<br/>```javascript<br/>const tile = layer.getTileAt(x, y);<br/>tile.alpha = 0.5; // Set to 50% transparency<br/>```<br/><br/>Note that while `TilemapLayer` itself has global alpha control inherited from `Phaser.GameObjects.Components.Alpha`,[1] this affects the entire layer. Per-tile alpha control is achieved through the individual `Tile` object's alpha property.[2] |

</details>
</tool-use>

The method accepts parameters for:<br/>- `tileX` - left most tile index<br/>- `tileY` - top most tile index  <br/>- `width` - how many tiles wide<br/>- `height` - how many tiles tall[3]<br/><br/>**FilteringOptions** - When retrieving tiles, you can filter results using options like:<br/>- `isNotEmpty` - only tiles with index != -1<br/>- `isColliding` - only colliding tiles<br/>- `hasInterestingFace` - only tiles with interesting faces[5] |

Phase 2.2 is implemented. Summary:

## Phase 2.2 — Field of View & Fog of War

### 1. **FOV config and state**
- **`gameConfig.ts`**: Added `FOV_RADIUS = 8`
- **`store.ts`**: Session state now has `exploredTiles: string[]` plus `addExploredTiles` and `clearExploredTiles`
- **`gameStore.ts`**: Implemented these actions and initialized `exploredTiles` as `[]`

### 2. **FOVSystem (`src/game/systems/FOVSystem.ts`)**
- Uses `ROT.FOV.PreciseShadowcasting` from rot-js
- `computeVisibleTiles(originX, originY, options)` returns a `Set<string>` of visible tile coordinates
- `lightPasses` lets light through floor tiles and blocks it on walls (`TILE.WALL`, `TILE.RUBBLE`)

### 3. **ExploreScene integration**
- On create: clears explored tiles and runs FOV from spawn
- On player move: recomputes FOV, updates explored tiles, and applies fog to all layers
- **Three visibility states**:
  - **Unexplored**: alpha 0 (black)
  - **Explored**: alpha 0.5 (dim)
  - **Visible**: alpha 1
- **`area-entered`** is emitted when a room first enters the FOV (tracked via `revealedRoomNames`)

### Flow
- Fog is applied to ground, wall, and decoration layers with per-tile alpha
- Explored tiles are stored in the session store for the current run
- Room discovery is announced when a room becomes visible for the first time (from corridors or by entering it)

Build completed successfully; you can test the flow in-game.

[Phaser - Examples - v3.55.0 - tilemap - Get Tiles In Shape](https://phaser.io/examples/v3.55.0/tilemap/view/get-tiles-in-shape)<br/>2. [Phaser.Tilemaps.Components \| Phaser Help](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/3.88.2/namespace/tilemaps-components)<br/>3. [Phaser.Tilemaps \| Phaser Help](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/3.88.2/function/tilemaps)<br/>4. [Phaser Dev Log 147](https://phaser.io/devlogs/147)<br/>5. [Public Members](https://docs.phaser.io/api-documentation/class/tilemaps-tile)<br/><br/># Phaser 3 forEachTile and Empty Tiles<br/><br/>## forEachTile Method<br/><br/>The `forEachTile` method is a Tilemap API function that allows you to iterate over tiles in a tilemap layer.[4] You can use it on Tilemap, StaticTilemapLayer, or DynamicTilemapLayer objects. In the example provided, it's used to modify tile properties during iteration:<br/><br/>```javascript<br/>map.forEachTile(function (tile) \{ tile.alpha = 1; \});<br/>```<br/><br/>## Handling Empty Tiles<br/><br/>When creating a tilemap from data, you can control how empty tiles (those with an index of -1) are handled using the `insertNull` parameter:[3]<br/><br/>- **`insertNull: true`** - Empty locations will be set to `null`. This is memory-efficient for large, sparsely populated maps where tile data doesn't need to change.<br/>- **`insertNull: false`** (default) - Empty locations will get a Tile object with an index of -1. This is better for small maps or when you need to update tiles dynamically.<br/><br/>## isNotEmpty<br/><br/>While not explicitly detailed in these results, the filtering of empty vs. non-empty tiles within `forEachTile` would typically involve checking the tile's index property within your callback function to determine if it's empty (index of -1) or contains a tile. |

</details>
</tool-use>

The change replaces `getTileAt` with `forEachTile`, which walks over each tile in the layer instead of indexing by coordinates.

`getTileAt` was failing because the wall and decoration layers are created from separate tilemap instances (`wallMapData`, `decoMapData`), so their internal layer data may not match what Phaser expects when it checks `layer.width`.

`forEachTile` uses the layer’s own tile list, so it works consistently across all three layers. It also skips iterating empty cells, which is fine because fog only needs to be applied where tiles exist.

[Graphics - Notes of Phaser 3](https://rexrainbow.github.io/phaser3-rex-notes/docs/site/graphics/)<br/><br/># Phaser 3 Graphics: lineBetween, lineStyle, and strokePath<br/><br/>## lineStyle<br/>Sets the line style for drawing. The method takes three parameters:[3]<br/>- `lineWidth`: The width of the line<br/>- `color`: The color in hex format (0xRRGGBB)<br/>- `alpha`: The transparency value (0-1)<br/><br/>Example: `graphics.lineStyle(5, 0xff00ff, 1.0);`[3]<br/><br/>## lineBetween<br/>Draws a line between two points. It takes four parameters:[1][5]<br/>- `x1, y1`: Starting point coordinates<br/>- `x2, y2`: Ending point coordinates<br/><br/>Example: `graphics.lineBetween(100, 100, 600, 500);`[1]<br/><br/>## strokePath<br/>Strokes (draws the outline of) a path that was defined using `beginPath()`, `moveTo()`, `lineTo()`, and `closePath()` methods.[2][3]<br/><br/>Example workflow:[2]<br/>```js<br/>graphics.lineStyle(16, 0x00ff00, 1);<br/>graphics.beginPath();<br/>graphics.moveTo(400, 100);<br/>graphics.lineTo(200, 278);<br/>graphics.lineTo(340, 430);<br/>graphics.closePath();<br/>graphics.strokePath();<br/>```<br/><br/>The `lineBetween()` method is a shortcut for drawing a single line, while the path-based approach (`beginPath()`, `moveTo()`, `lineTo()`, `strokePath()`) is used for drawing complex shapes with multiple connected lines.[3] |

---


Implemented a wall outline that draws thin borders along wall edges. Summary:

1. **2px dark warm brown (`#2a2520`)** – chosen to work on both floor and wall tiles.

2. **Edges only** – For each wall tile, a border is drawn only on edges between wall and walkable tiles, so walls are outlined without blocking interior tiles.

3. **Fog of war aware** – Outlines are only drawn for visible and explored tiles, and fade in explored areas (alpha 0.6 vs 1.0 in full view).

4. **Depth** – The outline is drawn at depth 2.5, above the decoration layer and below the player/interactives.

You should now see a clear boundary between walls and walkable tiles. If you want different thickness or color, those can be adjusted.

Here’s what changed to make the player easier to find:

## 1. **High-contrast texture** (BootScene)
- Switched from green (`0x88ff88`) to **cyan** (`0x0ec3c9`)
- Added a **4px dark outline** (`0x1a1a2e`, same as scene background)
- Switched to a circular shape instead of a rectangle

## 2. **Highlight ring** (Player)
- Added a white **stroke circle** (3px, 85% opacity) around the player
- Ring radius is 20px so it extends slightly beyond the sprite
- Ring is drawn under the player so it doesn’t cover the character

## 3. **Container-based layout**
- Player visuals are in a `Container` (ring + sprite)
- Camera follows the container so movement stays smooth

The player should now stand out more:
- **Cyan + dark outline** for contrast on brown/green tiles
- **White ring** for a clear “you are here” marker

You can tweak `HIGHLIGHT_RADIUS`, `HIGHLIGHT_COLOR`, and `HIGHLIGHT_ALPHA` in `Player.ts` if you want changes.

---


Here’s what changed:

## 1. **Switched fog to a Graphics overlay**

Fog is no longer applied by changing tile alphas on the layers. Instead, a `Graphics` object draws black rectangles over the map per tile:

- **Unexplored**: `fillRect` with alpha 1
- **Explored**: `fillRect` with alpha 0.5
- **Visible**: no drawing

This avoids Phaser’s tilemap APIs (`forEachTile`, `getTileAt`) on the wall and decoration layers, which were causing the `layer.width` error.

## 2. **Updated `.cursor/rules`**

Added a **“Phaser Tilemap Gotchas”** section:

- Avoid `forEachTile` / `getTileAt` on layers from separate tilemaps.
- Prefer fog overlays instead of per-tile alpha for fog of war.
- Note that using multiple `make.tilemap()` calls can be brittle for tile iteration.

This should cut down on similar Phaser tilemap errors. If you still see `layer.width`-type issues when adding new features, we can extend these rules.

The interaction prompt only appears when the player is **standing on** the interactive tile, not when they’re adjacent and facing it.

So `[E] Transporter pad` will show only when you’re on the transporter tile, and the same rule applies to book fragments and other interactives.

---



---


Updated the rules with these additions:

**Architecture**
- Visual accessibility note: high contrast, distinct visuals for legally blind users

**New patterns**
- **rot-js imports** – how to import and use Map, FOV, and lightPasses
- **Player entity** – Container structure, `getPixelPosition()` for UI, highlight ring kept for accessibility
- **Interaction system** – prompt only when standing ON the tile (no “tile in front” logic)
- **Depth ordering** – z-order for ExploreScene layers for placing new visuals
- **BootScene procedural textures** – `generateTexture` pattern for placeholder sprites
- **Session vs persisted state** – `exploredTiles` and `partialize`
- **EventBridge events** – list of event names and shapes

**Tilemap**
- Use raw `mapData.walls` when iterating, not Phaser tilemap layers

_**User**_

I would also like there to be more contrast between floor tiles and wall tiles, to make it more obvious. For example, in this screenshot, some of the floor tiles are as dark as the wall tiles. This makes it hard for me to tell which is which, even with the outline added.

That said, we do want some realism too. A room could have dark floors and white walls. So maybe there's other ways we could convey this information--maybe a thicker border that's white instead of black?

Don't code anything yet, just give me your thoughts and some options.

---

_**Agent (model claude-4.5-opus-high-thinking, mode Agent)**_



---


Here are some options that might work:

---

## 1. **Border color and thickness**

**White or light border on walls**  
Draw the existing wall outline in white or light gray instead of dark brown. Walls read as “solid” and walkable tiles stay visually open. Works even when both floor and wall are dark.

**Thicker white outline**  
Making the outline thicker (e.g. 2–3px → 4px) and light improves visibility.

**Trade-off:** On light walls, a white outline can blend in, so it helps most where floors and walls are both dark.

---

## 2. **Different treatments for floor vs. wall**

**Wall-only outline**  
Keep the current outline only on walls. Floors have no outline, so “has outline” = wall, “no outline” = floor. No extra color logic needed.

**Subtle walkable indicator**  
Add a very light tint or “walkable” hint on floor tiles (e.g. small dot, corner mark, or slight glow). Walls stay plain. Makes walkable vs. non-walkable distinguishable without forcing floor/wall colors to contrast.

---

## 3. **Use value instead of hue**

**Floor vs. wall by brightness, not color**  
Keep variety (brown floor, gray wall, etc.) but enforce a rule:  
- Walkable tiles: minimum brightness  
- Walls: maximum brightness  

So a dark floor never goes below a certain lightness, and walls never go above another threshold. They stay different but more readable.

---

## 4. **Visual texture difference**

**Texture or pattern on walls**  
Use a different texture/pattern for walls (e.g. blocks, mortar lines) even if the base color is close. Floors stay relatively uniform.

---

## 5. **Combine a few ideas**

- **Light outline on walls** (white or pale) + **thicker stroke** (3–4px).  
- Optionally: **minimum brightness floor rule** so dark floors stay distinguishable from walls.  
- Optional: subtle **walkable indicator** (e.g. a small dot or corner highlight on floors).

---

## Recommendation

**Primary approach:**  
- Switch the wall outline from dark brown/black to **white or light gray** and make it **3–4px** thick.  
- Walls read clearly as “bounded / solid,” and floors read as “unbounded / walkable.”

**Optional enhancement:**  
- Enforce **minimum floor brightness** so dark brown floors never get as dark as the darkest walls.  
- Small, subtle **floor marker** (e.g. faint corner dots) if needed for extra clarity.

I can help turn any of these into a concrete implementation plan or implementation steps when you’re ready.

---

Keep variety (brown floor, gray wall, etc.) but enforce a rule:
Walkable tiles: minimum brightness
Walls: maximum brightness
So a dark floor never goes below a certain lightness, and walls never go above another threshold. They stay different but more readable.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Removing tile borders**

I'm removing the black borders from the tile grid by modifying BootScene's strokeRect implementation. I'm mapping tile indices to their respective colors and identifying walkable floors vs walls for border removal. 

**Adjusting tile brightness**

I'm converting tile colors to luminance to ensure floor tiles aren't too dark and wall tiles aren't too light. I'm enforcing luminance ranges for floor and wall tiles to ensure proper contrast. I'm setting floor tile luminance threshold to 0.3 and capping wall tile luminance at 0.28. I'm adjusting wall tile luminance thresholds and will darken rubble from 0x5a5a5a to 0x4a4a4a or 0x454. I'm defining min floor luminance at 0.32 and max wall luminance at 0.28 to ensure consistent contrast. I'm expanding palette variety by adding more accent colors while maintaining contrast ratios. 

I'm adjusting the existing palette to enforce brightness gaps and ensure variety within current tile types. </details></think>

Here's what changed:

## 1. **Removed per-tile grid borders**
- Removed the `strokeRect` on each tileset tile in BootScene, so there are no borders between individual grid tiles.

## 2. **Lighter, thicker wall outlines**
- Wall outline color: dark brown (`0x2a2520`) → light warm white (`0xe8e4dc`).
- Stroke width: 2px → 4px.
- Walls read as bounded/solid; floors stay unbounded.

## 3. **Brightness rules for floor vs wall**
- **Dirt** (`0x6b4423` → `0x7a5530`): Lightened so it stays clearly above wall brightness.
- **Walls** (`0x3d3d3d` → `0x353535`, `0x5a5a5a` → `0x454545`): Slightly darkened to stay below floor luminance.
- **Debris** (`0x555555` → `0x4a4a4a`): Adjusted to fit the new range.

Palette variety (browns, greens, grays) is kept while enforcing a floor/wall brightness gap. Added a note about this in `.cursor/rules` so future palettes keep the same rules.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Updating tooltip contrast**

I'm adjusting tooltip contrast settings to meet WCAG 2.2 guidelines. I'm implementing a simplified contrast enforcement with floor tiles staying above a luminance threshold and walls below, ensuring clear walkable vs non-walkable distinctions. </details></think>

---


Keeping a clear separation:

- UI (tooltips, HUD, dialogue, menus): Enforce WCAG 2.2 AA (≥4.5:1 for normal text) — these are chrome with no artistic constraint.
- Game environment: Keep the existing floor/wall luminance gap. Full WCAG ratios would constrain palette variety; the floor-vs-wall rule preserves variety while keeping walkable vs. solid distinguishable. Frame it as "environment contrast rules that support accessibility without sacrificing variety."

[Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2](https://w3.org/TR/2024/REC-WCAG22-20241212)<br/>3. [Understanding Success Criterion 1.4.3: Contrast (Minimum) \| WAI \| W3C](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum.html)<br/>4. [Understanding<br/>     SC 1.4.3 :<br/>   Contrast (Minimum) (Level AA)](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/contrast-minimum)<br/>5. [Color Contrast Checker WCAG 2.2 — Free Tools & Ratios Guide 2026 \| Web Accessibility Checker \| Web Accessibility Checker](https://web-accessibility-checker.com/en/blog/color-contrast-checker-wcag-guide)<br/><br/># WCAG 2.2 AA Contrast Ratio: 4.5:1<br/><br/>## Standard Requirement<br/><br/>Under WCAG 2.2 Level AA, the standard contrast ratio requirement is **4.5:1** for normal text and images of text.[3][4] This means the visual presentation of text must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 between the text and its background.[3]<br/><br/>## Exceptions<br/><br/>There are specific exceptions to the 4.5:1 requirement:[3][4]<br/><br/>- **Large text**: Large-scale text (18 point or 14 point bold) requires only a 3:1 contrast ratio[3][4]<br/>- **Incidental text**: Text that is decorative, part of an inactive component, or part of a larger image has no contrast requirement[4]<br/>- **Logotypes**: Text in logos or brand names has no contrast requirement[3]<br/><br/>## Purpose<br/><br/>This requirement is designed to ensure text can be read by people with moderately low vision or impaired contrast perception, without needing contrast-enhancing assistive technology.[4] The contrast calculation focuses on luminance differences rather than color alone, so it works for people with color blindness as well.[4]<br/><br/>## Context<br/><br/>Level AA is the legal baseline for web accessibility in the EU and under US Section 508.[5] WCAG 2.2 was published as a W3C Recommendation in December 2024.[2] |

</details>
</tool-use>

Summary of changes:

## Tooltip contrast

- **Text**: `#cccccc` → `#ffffff`
- **Background**: `#00000099` (60% opacity) → `#0d0d14` (solid)
- **Padding**: 8px horizontal, 4px vertical for clearer framing

This gives roughly 15:1 contrast (above WCAG AAA 7:1).

## .cursor/rules update

New **WCAG 2.2 contrast (UI only)** section:

- **Tooltips, HUD, dialogue, menus**: WCAG 2.2 AA (≥4.5:1 for normal text, ≥3:1 for large text). Solid backgrounds preferred.
- **Game environment**: Uses the existing floor/wall brightness rules, not full WCAG, to keep palette flexibility for biomes and terrain.

Environment rules stay separate from UI so variety stays high while UI stays readable.

Current status:

- **Phase 1** – Done (setup, store, map, player, interaction, React shell)
- **Phase 2.1** – Done (procedural map generation)
- **Phase 2.2** – Done (FOV, fog of war, explored tiles, area-entered, wall outlines, player visibility, tooltip contrast, WCAG rules)

**Next: Phase 2.3 — Book Fragment Placement & Collection**

Planned work for 2.3:

1. **Data model** – Define `Book` and `Fragment` in `src/data/books.ts`
2. **Book catalog** – 3–5 books with real Gutenberg text (Inferno, Tempest, Canterbury Tales, Faerie Queene, Dickinson)
3. **BookPickup entity** – Glow sprite, interaction type `book`, emit `book-found`, add to store
4. **Placement** – 2–4 fragments per map in rooms, random open tiles, no duplicates in a room, at least one far from spawn

We already have one book pickup in ExploreScene; the goal is to wire it into the real book system and add proper placement.

---

_**User**_

Would like to add Bible chapters to the book fragments, I guess King James would be good for flavor, plus it's probably available on Gutenberg. Even though as an Episcopalian I vastly prefer NRSV-UE and have some big problems with KJV. But the language of KJV fits the tone of the game of trying to preserve old stuff.

---

_**Agent (model claude-4.5-opus-high-thinking, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Considering design choices**

I'm noting the thematic alignment of using KJV for the game's 'preserving old stuff' concept. 

**Confirming bible text source**

I'm confirming that KJV is available on Gutenberg and noting it fits the theme of preserving old texts. I'm noting that KJV is on Gutenberg and will suggest including iconic chapters like Genesis 1, Psalms 23, and Revelation excerpts in the catalog. </details></think>

---


KJV fits the theme well: old, literary, preservation-focused, and very much in the “recovering lost texts” spirit of the game.

King James is on Project Gutenberg in multiple forms — full text and by book — so it’s easy to pull from.

**Bible structure** – chapters are a natural fragment unit. With 66 books and ~1,189 chapters, a few options:

- **Iconic chapters**: Genesis 1, Psalms 23, Matthew 5–7 (Sermon on the Mount), 1 Corinthians 13, Revelation 21, etc.
- **By book**: Genesis, Psalms, one Gospel, Revelation
- **Mixed**: A handful of well-known chapters across OT and NT

I’d lean toward **5–8 iconic chapters** as fragments: recognizable, good length, and strong flavor.

If you want this folded into Phase 2.3, we can add “King James Bible (selected chapters)” to the book catalog next to Inferno, Tempest, Chaucer, Faerie Queene, and Dickinson. Want me to add it to the plan explicitly and include it when we implement 2.3?

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Creating data file**

I'm creating src/data/books.ts with the book catalog including fragment types and representative excerpts. I'm adding fragment IDs to book entries and updating the interaction system to use them for fragment lookup. 

**Updating interaction system**

I'm modifying the GameContainer to handle book interactions by fetching fragments and triggering the reading phase. I'm implementing a close button for book reading that will trigger the store's closeDialogue function to reset the game phase. I'm implementing a closeBook function that resets isReadingBook, currentBookFragment, and gamePhase to 'exploring' when closing a book. 

**Placing books in catalog**

I'm placing books randomly in catalog rooms with unique fragment IDs and storing sprite references for later destruction. I'm implementing book destruction logic by having ExploreScene track interactive IDs and their associated sprites for cleanup. 

**Tracking book sprites**

I'm creating a Map to track book sprites for proper cleanup during destruction. I'm implementing event listeners for book collection to handle sprite cleanup in the scene. </details></think>

  "markdown": "The Project Gutenberg eBook of The Bible, King James version, Book 1: Genesis \n \nThis ebook is for the use of anyone anywhere in the United States and \nmost other parts of the world at no cost and with almost no restrictions \nwhatsoever. You may copy it, give it away or re-use it under the terms \nof the Project Gutenberg License included with this ebook or online \nat www.gutenberg.org. If you are not located in the United States, \nyou will have to check the laws of the country where you are located \nbefore using this eBook. \n \nTitle: The Bible, King James version, Book 1: Genesis \n \nAuthor: Anonymous \n \nRelease date: April 1, 2005 [eBook #8001] \n Most recently updated: December 26, 2020 \n \nLanguage: English \n \nCredits: This eBook was produced by David Widger with the help of Derek Andrew's text from January 1992 and the work of Bryan Taylor in November 2002 \n \n \n*** START OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK THE BIBLE, KING JAMES VERSION, BOOK 1: GENESIS *** \n \n \n \n \nThis eBook was produced by David Widger \nwith the help of Derek Andrew's text from January 1992 \nand the work of Bryan Taylor in November 2002. \n \n \n \n \n \nBook 01 Genesis \n \n01:001:001 In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth. \n \n01:001:002 And the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was \n upon the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God moved upon \n the face of the waters. \n \n01:001:003 And God said, Let there be light: and there was light. \n \n01:001:004 And God saw the light, that it was good: and God divided the \n light from the darkness. \n \n01:001:005 And God called the light Day, and the darkness he called \n Night. And the evening and the morning were the first day. \n \n01:001:006 And God said, Let there be a firmament in the midst of the \n waters, and let it divide the waters from the waters. \n \n01:001:007 And God made the firmament, and divided the waters which were \n under the firmament from the waters which were above the \n firmament: and it was so. \n \n01:001:008 And God called the firmament Heaven. And the evening and the \n morning were the second day. \n \n01:001:009 And God said, Let the waters under the heaven be gathered \n together unto one place, and let the dry land appear: and it \n was so. \n \n01:001:010 And God called the dry land Earth; and the gathering together \n of the waters called he Seas: and God saw that it was good. \n \n01:001:011 And God said, Let the earth bring forth grass, the herb \n yielding seed, and the fruit tree yielding fruit after his \n kind, whose seed is in itself, upon the earth: and it was so. \n \n01:001:012 And the earth brought forth grass, and herb yielding seed \n after his kind, and the tree yielding fruit, whose seed was in \n itself, after his kind: and God saw that it was good. \n \n01:001:013 And the evening and the morning were the third day. \n \n01:001:014 And God said, Let there be lights in the firmament of the \n heaven to divide the day from the night; and let them be for \n signs, and for seasons, and for days, and years: \n \n01:001:015 And let them be for lights in the firmament of the heaven to \n give light upon the earth: and it was so. \n \n01:001:016 And God made two great lights; the greater light to rule the \n day, and the lesser light to rule the night: he made the stars \n also. \n \n01:001:017 And God set them in the firmament of the heaven to give light \n upon the earth, \n \n01:001:018 And to rule over the day and over the night, and to divide the \n light from the darkness: and God saw that it was good. \n \n01:001:019 And the evening and the morning were the fourth day. \n \n01:001:020 And God said, Let the waters bring forth abundantly the moving \n creature that hath life, and fowl that may fly above the earth \n in the open firmament of heaven. \n \n01:001:021 And God created great whales, and every living creature that \n moveth, which the waters brought forth abundantly, after their \n kind, and every winged fowl after his kind: and God saw that \n it was good. \n \n01:001:022 And God blessed them, saying, Be fruitful, and multiply, and \n fill the waters in the seas, and let fowl multiply in the \n earth. \n \n01:001:023 And the evening and the morning were the fifth day. \n \n01:001:024 And God said, Let the earth bring forth the living creature \n after his kind, cattle, and creeping thing, and beast of the \n earth after his kind: and it was so. \n \n01:001:025 And God made the beast of the earth after his kind, and cattle \n after their kind, and every thing that creepeth upon the earth \n after his kind: and God saw that it was good. \n \n01:001:026 And God said, Let us make man in our image, after our \n likeness: and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, \n and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over \n all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creepeth \n upon the earth. \n \n01:001:027 So God created man in his own image, in the image of God \n created he him; male and female created he them. \n \n01:001:028 And God blessed them, and God said unto them, Be fruitful, and \n multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it: and have \n dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the \n air, and over every living thing that moveth upon the earth. \n \n01:001:029 And God said, Behold, I have given you every herb bearing \n seed, which is upon the face of all the earth, and every tree, \n in the which is the fruit of a tree yielding seed; to you it \n shall be for meat. \n \n01:001:030 And to every beast of the earth, and to every fowl of the air, \n and to every thing that creepeth upon the earth, wherein there \n is life, I have given every green herb for meat: and it was \n so. \n \n01:001:031 And God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was \n very good. And the evening and the morning were the sixth day. \n \n01:002:001 Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host \n of them. \n \n01:002:002 And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; \n and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he \n had made. \n \n01:002:003 And God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it: because \n that in it he had rested from all his work which God created \n and made. \n \n01:002:004 These are the generations of the heavens and of the earth when \n they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth \n and the heavens, \n \n01:002:005 And every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and \n every herb of the field before it grew: for the LORD God had \n not caused it to rain upon the earth, and there was not a man \n to till the ground. \n \n01:002:006 But there went up a mist from the earth, and watered the whole \n face of the ground. \n \n01:002:007 And the LORD God formed man of the dust of the ground, and \n breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became \n a living soul. \n \n01:002:008 And the LORD God planted a garden eastward in Eden; and there \n he put the man whom he had formed. \n \n01:002:009 And out of the ground made the LORD God to grow every tree \n that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of \n life also in the midst of the garden, and the tree of \n knowledge of good and evil. \n \n01:002:010 And a river went out of Eden to water the garden; and from \n thence it was parted, and became into four heads. \n \n01:002:011 The name of the first is Pison: that is it which compasseth \n the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold; \n \n01:002:012 And the gold of that land is good: there is bdellium and the \n onyx stone. \n \n01:002:013 And the name of the second river is Gihon: the same is it that \n compasseth the whole land of Ethiopia. \n \n01:002:014 And the name of the third river is Hiddekel: that is it which \n goeth toward the east of Assyria. And the fourth river is \n Euphrates. \n \n01:002:015 And the LORD God took the man, and put him into the garden of \n Eden to dress it and to keep it. \n \n01:002:016 And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of \n the garden thou mayest freely eat: \n \n01:002:017 But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt \n not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou \n shalt surely die. \n \n01:002:018 And the LORD God said, It is not good that the man should be \n alone; I will make him an help meet for him. \n \n01:002:019 And out of the ground the LORD God formed every beast of the \n field, and every fowl of the air; and brought them unto Adam \n to see what he would call them: and whatsoever Adam called \n every living creature, that was the name thereof. \n \n01:002:020 And Adam gave names to all cattle, and to the fowl of the air, \n and to every beast of the field; but for Adam there was not \n found an help meet for him. \n \n01:002:021 And the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon Adam, and he \n slept: and he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh \n instead thereof; \n \n01:002:022 And the rib, which the LORD God had taken from man, made he a \n woman, and brought her unto the man. \n \n01:002:023 And Adam said, This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my \n flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of \n Man. \n \n01:002:024 Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and \n shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh. \n \n01:002:025 And they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not \n ashamed. \n \n01:003:001 Now the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field \n which the LORD God had made. And he said unto the woman, Yea, \n hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden? \n \n01:003:002 And the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit \n of the trees of the garden: \n \n01:003:003 But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the \n garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall \n ye touch it, lest ye die. \n \n01:003:004 And the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die: \n \n01:003:005 For God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your \n eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good \n and evil. \n \n01:003:006 And when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and \n that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to \n make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and \n gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat. \n \n01:003:007 And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they \n were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together, and made \n themselves aprons. \n \n01:003:008 And they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden \n in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves \n from the presence of the LORD God amongst the trees of the \n garden. \n \n01:003:009 And the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where \n art thou? \n \n01:003:010 And he said, I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was \n afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself. \n \n01:003:011 And he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou \n eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou \n shouldest not eat? \n \n01:003:012 And the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, \n she gave me of the tree, and I did eat. \n \n01:003:013 And the LORD God said unto the woman, What is this that thou \n hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I \n did eat. \n \n01:003:014 And the LORD God said unto the serpent, Because thou hast done \n this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast \n of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt \n thou eat all the days of thy life: \n \n01:003:015 And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between \n thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou \n shalt bruise his heel. \n \n01:003:016 Unto the woman he said, I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and \n thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and \n thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over \n thee. \n \n01:003:017 And unto Adam he said, Because thou hast hearkened unto the \n voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I \n commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it: cursed is \n the ground for thy sake; in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all \n the days of thy life; \n \n01:003:018 Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and \n thou shalt eat the herb of the field; \n \n01:003:019 In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou \n return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken: for \n dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return. \n \n01:003:020 And Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the \n mother of all living. \n \n01:003:021 Unto Adam also and to his wife did the LORD God make coats of \n skins, and clothed them. \n \n01:003:022 And the LORD God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, \n to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, \n and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever: \n \n01:003:023 Therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, \n to till the ground from whence he was taken. \n \n01:003:024 So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the \n garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned \n every way, to keep the way of the tree of life. \n \n01:004:001 And Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, \n and said, I have gotten a man from the LORD. \n \n01:004:002 And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of \n sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground. \n \n01:004:003 And in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of \n the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD. \n \n01:004:004 And Abel, he also brought of the firstlings of his flock and \n of the fat thereof. And the LORD had respect unto Abel and to \n his offering: \n \n01:004:005 But unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain \n was very wroth, and his countenance fell. \n \n01:004:006 And the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is \n thy countenance fallen? \n \n01:004:007 If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou \n doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be \n his desire, and thou shalt rule over him. \n \n01:004:008 And Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, \n when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel \n his brother, and slew him. \n \n01:004:009 And the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he \n said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper? \n \n01:004:010 And he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's \n blood crieth unto me from the ground. \n \n01:004:011 And now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her \n mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand; \n \n01:004:012 When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield \n unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou \n be in the earth. \n \n01:004:013 And Cain said unto the LORD, My punishment is greater than I \n can bear. \n \n01:004:014 Behold, thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the \n earth; and from thy face shall I be hid; and I shall be a \n fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to \n pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me. \n \n01:004:015 And the LORD said unto him, Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, \n vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the LORD set a \n mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him. \n \n01:004:016 And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in \n the land of Nod, on the east of Eden. \n \n01:004:017 And Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch: and \n he builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the \n name of his son, Enoch. \n \n01:004:018 And unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and \n Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech. \n \n01:004:019 And Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was \n Adah, and the name of the other Zillah. \n \n01:004:020 And Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in \n tents, and of such as have cattle. \n \n01:004:021 And his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all \n such as handle the harp and organ. \n \n01:004:022 And Zillah, she also bare Tubalcain, an instructor of every \n artificer in brass and iron: and the sister of Tubalcain was \n Naamah. \n \n01:004:023 And Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my \n voice; ye wives of Lamech, hearken unto my speech: for I have \n slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt. \n \n01:004:024 If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and \n sevenfold. \n \n01:004:025 And Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called \n his name Seth: For God, said she, hath appointed me another \n seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew. \n \n01:004:026 And to Seth, to him also there was born a son; and he called \n his name Enos: then began men to call upon the name of the \n LORD. \n \n01:005:001 This is the book of the generations of Adam. In the day that \n God created man, in the likeness of God made he him; \n \n01:005:002 Male and female created he them; and blessed them, and called \n their name Adam, in the day when they were created. \n \n01:005:003 And Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son in \n his own likeness, and after his image; and called his name \n Seth: \n \n01:005:004 And the days of Adam after he had begotten Seth were eight \n hundred years: and he begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:005 And all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty \n years: and he died. \n \n01:005:006 And Seth lived an hundred and five years, and begat Enos: \n \n01:005:007 And Seth lived after he begat Enos eight hundred and seven \n years, and begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:008 And all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years: \n and he died. \n \n01:005:009 And Enos lived ninety years, and begat Cainan: \n \n01:005:010 And Enos lived after he begat Cainan eight hundred and fifteen \n years, and begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:011 And all the days of Enos were nine hundred and five years: and \n he died. \n \n01:005:012 And Cainan lived seventy years and begat Mahalaleel: \n \n01:005:013 And Cainan lived after he begat Mahalaleel eight hundred and \n forty years, and begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:014 And all the days of Cainan were nine hundred and ten years: \n and he died. \n \n01:005:015 And Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years, and begat Jared: \n \n01:005:016 And Mahalaleel lived after he begat Jared eight hundred and \n thirty years, and begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:017 And all the days of Mahalaleel were eight hundred ninety and \n five years: and he died. \n \n01:005:018 And Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years, and he begat \n Enoch: \n \n01:005:019 And Jared lived after he begat Enoch eight hundred years, and \n begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:020 And all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two \n years: and he died. \n \n01:005:021 And Enoch lived sixty and five years, and begat Methuselah: \n \n01:005:022 And Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three \n hundred years, and begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:023 And all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five \n years: \n \n01:005:024 And Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him. \n \n01:005:025 And Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years, and \n begat Lamech. \n \n01:005:026 And Methuselah lived after he begat Lamech seven hundred \n eighty and two years, and begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:027 And all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty and \n nine years: and he died. \n \n01:005:028 And Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years, and begat a \n son: \n \n01:005:029 And he called his name Noah, saying, This same shall comfort \n us concerning our work and toil of our hands, because of the \n ground which the LORD hath cursed. \n \n01:005:030 And Lamech lived after he begat Noah five hundred ninety and \n five years, and begat sons and daughters: \n \n01:005:031 And all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy and \n seven years: and he died. \n \n01:005:032 And Noah was five hundred years old: and Noah begat Shem, Ham, \n and Japheth. \n \n01:006:001 And it came to pass, when men began to multiply on the face of \n the earth, and daughters were born unto them, \n \n01:006:002 That the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were \n fair; and they took them wives of all which they chose. \n \n01:006:003 And the LORD said, My spirit shall not always strive with man, \n for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred \n and twenty years. \n \n01:006:004 There were giants in the earth in those days; and also after \n that, when the sons of God came in unto the daughters of men, \n and they bare children to them, the same became mighty men \n which were of old, men of renown. \n \n01:006:005 And God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, \n and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was \n only evil continually. \n \n01:006:006 And it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, \n and it grieved him at his heart. \n \n01:006:007 And the LORD said, I will destroy man whom I have created from \n the face of the earth; both man, and beast, and the creeping \n thing, and the fowls of the air; for it repenteth me that I \n have made them. \n \n01:006:008 But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD. \n \n01:006:009 These are the generations of Noah: Noah was a just man and \n perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God. \n \n01:006:010 And Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth. \n \n01:006:011 The earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was \n filled with violence. \n \n01:006:012 And God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt; \n for all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth. \n \n01:006:013 And God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before \n me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, \n behold, I will destroy them with the earth. \n
